Study A · Assessment of mesoscopic solvers

Source of spurious currents

The capillary force is a discretised stress divergence added in the collision step; on a standing-bubble (Young–Laplace) test an anisotropic force squares the bubble, while the isotropic D2Q9 stencil restores a circular shape with bounded parasitic velocity.

Spurious currents are non-physical velocities near a curved interface where the fluid should be at rest. Their source is the discretisation of the capillary force.

Where the force enters: collision, not streaming

Each step is a local collision then a streaming (transport). The Korteweg force is added to the collision as a source \(S_i\) (exact-difference method, co-moving frame); the post-collision \(f_i^{*}\) is what streams.

$$ f_i^{*} = \underbrace{f_i - \omega\,(f_i - f_i^{\,\mathrm{eq}})}_{\text{collision}} + \underbrace{S_i}_{\text{Korteweg source}}, \qquad S_i = \mathcal{G}^{\,u}_{\,u+\delta u}[\rho W_i] - \rho W_i, \quad \delta u = \frac{F\,\delta t}{\rho}. $$
  • Why forcing, not pressure-based: altering the equilibrium to carry the surface tension is not Galilean-invariant; adding it as a source \(S_i\) is more stable and admits large density ratios.
The force and its isotropic discretisation

The force is the divergence of the Korteweg stress; \(\kappa\) sets the surface tension.

$$ K_{\alpha\beta} = \kappa\!\left(\Delta\rho + \tfrac{1}{2}|\nabla\rho|^2\right)\delta_{\alpha\beta} + \kappa\,\partial_\alpha\rho\,\partial_\beta\rho, \qquad F_\alpha = \partial_\beta K_{\alpha\beta}. $$
  • Origin of the error: lattice gradients are not perfectly isotropic, so \(F\) does not exactly balance the pressure gradient at a curved interface → residual flow.
  • Fix: evaluate \(\nabla\rho\) with the isotropic D2Q9 stencil (Shan 2008), using the diagonal as well as cardinal neighbours → rotationally-invariant leading error.
Test: standing bubble = Young–Laplace test
  • Single bubble at equilibrium in a periodic box, zero initial velocity, no driving.
  • Exact solution is at rest, so any residual \(\max|\mathbf{u}|\) is pure discretisation error.
  • Good model: velocity small and bounded, bubble stays circular.
Anisotropic force → square bubble Isotropic stencil → circular bubble
Residual current — further suppression
A bounded residual current persists at the interface; relevant at high density ratios.
  • Higher isotropy order — wider stencils lower the leading error.
  • Finer interface resolution — more cells across the interface reduce the per-cell gradient error.

Korteweg stress, EDM forcing, two-population model: Reyhanian, PhD thesis Ch. 6 (Eqs. 6.28–6.30). Isotropic stencil: Shan 2008. Post-collision source, \(\kappa=0.06\): Study A note.

Study A · Assessment of mesoscopic solvers — companion to the candidacy presentation. Sources: Study A vault note; Reyhanian PhD thesis, Ch. 6. Equations via MathJax.
